game_title: A Text Adventure in Deep Space
Where the Stars Don't Care and Neither Does the Sheriff
intro: The Bellerophon slides into Berth Seven with the practiced grace of a ship that
knows it's not welcome anywhere in particular and has made its peace with that.
Dustpocket Station. LV-7 sector. End of the line, or near enough.
Through the viewport, the station looks the way it always looks: functional,
uninspiring, exactly the kind of place where something interesting is about to
happen to someone. Today, that someone is you.
The docking clamps engage. The airlock cycles. The universe, as it so often does,
fails to provide a welcome mat.
Type HELP for a list of commands. Type LOOK to see where you are.
Your score will be displayed with SCORE. Progress is its own reward,
help_text: GO [direction]        -- Move. N, S, E, W, U, D also work.
USE [item] ON [thing] -- Use an item on something specific
OPEN [thing]          -- Open a door, container, or hatch
GIVE [item] TO [person] -- Give an item to someone
ENTER [code] ON [thing] -- Enter a code on a lock
BOARD                 -- Board your ship (from Docking Bay 7)
The frontier rewards thoroughness. EXAMINE everything. TALK to everyone.
The universe will not volunteer information. Make it.

default: The universe would like you to know that it does not take this personally.
It takes nothing personally. That is, in many ways, the problem.
vacuum: You have died by exposure to the vacuum of space.
This is one of space's more reliable ways of making a point. The point is:
pressure suits exist for a reason. The reason is this.
combat: You have died in a manner that the frontier would describe as 'instructive.'
The lesson is: negotiations work better when you've identified your
leverage before entering the room. You had not identified your leverage.
hatch: You have died by falling through a hatch.
The hatch was labeled DO NOT OPEN for several reasons, the primary one being
that it opens over a significant drop. The warning system considered this
self-explanatory. The warning system was wrong about this.

inventory_empty: You are carrying nothing. This is either the start of a fresh beginning or the end of a very long story about things you've lost. Possibly both.
cannot_go: You can't go that way. The universe is many things, but cooperative about direction is not usually one of them.
cannot_take: That doesn't seem to be something you can take. Some things are fixed in place by physics, bureaucracy, or the quiet weight of being too large.
cannot_take_weight: You'd be carrying too much. There is a physical limit to how much the frontier will let you haul around, and you've reached it.
locked: It's locked. Locked things on the frontier are locked for reasons. Usually good ones.
wrong_code: That's not the right combination. The lock remains unimpressed.
npc_not_here: There's nobody by that name here.
unknown_command: You consider the words you just used and find them wanting. The universe declines to parse them. Try something else, or type HELP.
unknown_topic: They don't seem to have anything to say about that specifically. Ask about something more relevant to the immediate situation.
save_failed: The save failed. The universe, characteristically, offers no explanation.
load_success: Game loaded. You are back where you were, with everything you had.
load_failed: That save doesn't exist, or can't be read. The frontier is not sentimental about preservation.
quit_confirm: Are you sure you want to quit? Your progress will not be saved automatically. (YES/NO)
quit_message:
The Bellerophon's docking clamps disengage. The station recedes. The stars remain indifferent, as is their habit.

Thank you for playing.
footnotes_on: Footnotes enabled. The narrator's asides will now appear.
footnotes_off: Footnotes disabled. The narrator's asides will be suppressed, which the narrator accepts with professional resignation.
narrator_quips: The frontier waits. It is very good at this.
Somewhere, a recycled-air ventilation system hums to itself.
The stars outside the viewport continue to not care.
The station keeps its own counsel.
Everything that happens here is someone's fault. This is not a moral judgment — it is simply how causality works.
examine_self: You look the way you always look: like someone who has been places and is currently between them. Your clothes are functional. Your expression is noncommittal. You have been told you have 'the kind of face people remember when they need to and forget when they don't,' which you have decided to take as a compliment.
give_not_here: There's nobody by that name here.
give_no_effect: They accept this with polite confusion. Nothing changes.
board_no_codicil: The Bellerophon is ready when you are. But you're not ready yet. There's still something unresolved about this station that you can't quite leave behind.

sheriff: You place the Quantum Codicil on Sheriff Vasquez's desk.
She looks at it for a long moment. Then she picks it up, turns it over in her hands,
and sets it down again with the careful attention of someone handling something they've
"Two thousand credits," she says. "As promised." She transfers the payment without
ceremony. "I'll have the Codicil in secure custody within the hour. The claim goes
back to contested status pending court review — which means the Brotherhood gets their
day in court and the Concern gets their lawyers." A pause. "It's not a clean ending.
The frontier doesn't do clean. But it's a lawful one."
She looks up at you. "Good work, drifter. Don't spend it all in the Rusty Spur."
The Bellerophon leaves Dustpocket Station with two thousand credits in the account
and the particular satisfaction of having done the right thing in a place where
doing the right thing was neither easy nor rewarded except by itself.
syndicate: You set the Quantum Codicil on Director Holloway's desk.
She doesn't reach for it immediately. She looks at it, then at you, with the
"Interesting choice," she says, finally. "Most people who come through that door
have already decided who they're working for." She picks up the Codicil. "Five
thousand credits. As offered." The payment clears before she finishes the sentence.
"The Syndicate will file the appropriate paperwork. The Codicil goes into escrow
pending the Concern's review — which means it goes nowhere for another decade,
probably." She meets your eyes. "You know that. You chose it anyway."
You did. Stability has a cost, and sometimes the cost is justice deferred.
The Bellerophon departs with five thousand credits and the knowledge that Dustpocket
Station will continue to function, frictions maintained at sustainable levels,
factions balanced, nothing resolved and nothing exploding.
Director Holloway watches your ship go from the viewport of her underground office.
She does not wave. She was not expecting you to wave either.
brotherhood: You hand the Quantum Codicil to Augusto Claim.
He takes it slowly. Turns it over. His expression doesn't change for a long moment,
and then it changes completely — something like relief, something like vindication,
something that a harder man would have called hope and Augusto just calls correct.
"I'm going to file this with the Regional Claims Authority tomorrow morning," he says.
"The lawyers are going to fight it for six months. We're going to win." He looks at
you. "I don't have five thousand credits. I don't have two thousand. What I have is—"
"Keep it," you say. You didn't come out here for credits.
Augusto nods, once, like a man who has been waiting his whole life for someone to
say exactly that in exactly that tone.
The Bellerophon leaves Dustpocket Station with considerably fewer credits than it
might have had. Three weeks later, a regional court will rule in the Brotherhood's
favor on the LV-7-Gamma extraction rights. The miners will call the asteroid
Clementine, after no one in particular and everyone in general.
You'll hear about it, eventually. Out here, good news travels slow but it travels.
keep: The Quantum Codicil sits in your inside pocket. It's small — the size of a data
crystal, which is what it is. A title deed to an asteroid. A piece of evidence.
A future, compressed into something small enough to carry.
You didn't come to Dustpocket Station for this. You didn't come for any of it.
The frontier doesn't ask what you came for.
The docking clamps disengage. Dustpocket Station recedes through the viewport:
a collection of lights against the dark, smaller every second, then gone.
The Codicil in your pocket could go to a dozen buyers on a dozen stations.
Or it could stay there. Some things are worth more carried than sold.
The Bellerophon accelerates into the between-places, where you have always
been most comfortable, and the universe — characteristically — declines to judge.
